cards.title: HashMap Internals
cards.explanation: A HashMap stores key-value pairs in an array of buckets. The bucket index is computed from the key's hashCode(). When two keys hash to the same bucket (collision), Java uses a linked list (and a red-black tree when the list exceeds 8 entries, since Java 8). The default load factor is 0.75 — when 75% of buckets are filled, the table doubles in size and rehashes all entries.
cards.whyItMatters: HashMap is the most used data structure in Java after arrays. Understanding internals lets you tune initial capacity, avoid rehashing on large loads, and debug subtle equals/hashCode bugs.
cards.gotcha: If you override equals() you MUST override hashCode() consistently — objects that are equal must have the same hash code. Failing to do this breaks HashMap, HashSet, and any hash-based collection.
cards.vsComparison: Use HashMap for O(1) get/put with no ordering. Use LinkedHashMap to preserve insertion order. Use TreeMap for sorted key order (O(log n) operations).

cards.title: equals() and hashCode() Contract
cards.explanation: The Java equals/hashCode contract has two rules: (1) if a.equals(b) then a.hashCode() == b.hashCode() must be true, and (2) hashCode() must be consistent — calling it multiple times on the same object must return the same value as long as the object hasn't changed. Breaking rule 1 makes objects impossible to find in HashMap/HashSet after insertion.
cards.whyItMatters: This contract is one of the most common sources of subtle bugs in Java — objects disappear silently from sets and maps.
cards.gotcha: Using a mutable field in hashCode() is dangerous — if you mutate the field after inserting the object into a HashSet, you'll never find it again because the hash changed.

cards.title: String vs StringBuilder vs StringBuffer
cards.explanation: String is immutable — every concatenation creates a new object. StringBuilder is mutable and not thread-safe, making it the right choice for building strings in a single thread. StringBuffer is identical to StringBuilder but all methods are synchronized, making it thread-safe but slower. In practice, prefer StringBuilder; StringBuffer is rarely needed since better concurrency tools exist.
cards.whyItMatters: String concatenation in a loop is one of the most common performance bugs in Java. StringBuilder is the standard fix.
cards.gotcha: The compiler auto-optimises String + in a single expression, but NOT across loop iterations. Only the loop case is actually slow.
cards.vsComparison: String for immutable values, StringBuilder for single-threaded construction, StringBuffer only when truly needed across threads (rare — use other approaches instead).

cards.title: Array vs ArrayList
cards.explanation: A Java array is a fixed-size, contiguous block of memory that can hold primitives or objects. ArrayList is a resizable array backed by an Object[] that doubles in capacity when full (amortised O(1) add). Use a raw array when size is known and fixed, or when you need primitive performance (no boxing). Use ArrayList when you need dynamic resizing, the Collections API, or interoperability with streams.
cards.whyItMatters: Knowing when to use each avoids unnecessary boxing overhead and unexpected resizing costs in hot paths.
cards.gotcha: Arrays.asList() returns a fixed-size list backed by the array — you can set elements but not add or remove. Wrap it in new ArrayList<>() if you need a truly mutable list.
cards.vsComparison: Array: fixed size, primitives, fastest random access. ArrayList: dynamic size, objects only, slightly more memory due to boxing and Object[] overhead.

cards.title: LinkedList vs ArrayList
cards.explanation: LinkedList is a doubly-linked list where each node holds a value and references to the previous and next nodes. It has O(1) insert/remove at head or tail but O(n) random access. ArrayList has O(1) random access but O(n) insert/remove in the middle. In practice, ArrayList outperforms LinkedList for almost all workloads due to CPU cache locality — sequential traversal of an array is much faster than following heap pointers.
cards.whyItMatters: Choosing LinkedList expecting faster inserts often makes things slower due to GC pressure and cache misses. Benchmark before assuming.
cards.gotcha: Java's LinkedList implements both List and Deque. When you need a queue or stack, use ArrayDeque instead — it's faster and uses less memory.
cards.vsComparison: ArrayList: fast random access, cache-friendly, slow mid-list inserts. LinkedList: fast head/tail ops, poor cache locality, high memory overhead (two pointers per node).
